name: Chronic and Longitudinal Care Expansion Pack
description: Expanded local offline records for chronic disease monitoring, home follow-up, specialist review, longitudinal safety boundaries, and day-to-day care preparation.
reviewStatus: local pack requires clinician, pharmacy, and safety review before production medical use

records.title: Hypertension Home Trend and Symptom Review
records.summary: Organizes hypertension follow-up using trend patterns, repeat home readings, symptom timing, medication adherence, salt intake, hydration, sleep, and exercise context before the next safe step.
records.safetyNotes: Chest pain, severe headache, new weakness, breathing trouble, confusion, or very high readings with symptoms need urgent real-world medical review.
whatToTrack: At least two or three seated home readings with time, symptoms, and medicine timing.
Salt intake, stress, caffeine, hydration, exercise, and sleep changes around higher readings.
Any headache, vision change, dizziness, chest discomfort, or breathlessness.
careQuestions: Does the trend suggest a stable pattern, poor technique, or a need for sooner clinician review?
Which symptom and reading combinations should move this from routine monitoring to urgent assessment?
precautions: Do not change blood-pressure medicine doses from app guidance alone.
Do not rely on one isolated reading when symptoms or repeat values suggest a higher-risk pattern.

records.title: Low Blood Sugar Symptom and Recovery Review
records.summary: Builds a low-sugar review around symptoms, recent food intake, glucose readings, diabetes medicines, activity, alcohol, illness, and recovery pattern while keeping safety boundaries clear.
records.safetyNotes: Confusion, inability to swallow, fainting, seizure, repeated low glucose, or no improvement after fast-acting carbohydrate needs urgent real-world care.
whatToTrack: Exact glucose reading if available, symptoms, and whether the person can safely eat or drink.
Recent meals, insulin or diabetes medicine timing, activity, alcohol, vomiting, or infection.
How symptoms changed after treatment and whether the glucose improved on repeat check.
careQuestions: What likely triggered the low reading pattern and what should be reviewed with the diabetes team?
Which recurrence or safety signals mean this should move out of routine self-monitoring?
precautions: Do not drive or stay alone if symptoms suggest severe low blood sugar.
Do not make insulin dose changes from this app without clinician guidance.

records.title: Diabetes Foot, Wound, and Skin Check Review
records.summary: Structures diabetic foot questions around numbness, skin change, wounds, swelling, drainage, glucose control, shoe pressure, and walking limits before clinician follow-up.
records.safetyNotes: Spreading redness, pus, fever, worsening swelling, black skin, severe pain, or inability to bear weight needs urgent medical review.
whatToTrack: Exact wound or skin location, redness, drainage, swelling, pain, and walking impact.
Any numbness, prior ulcers, glucose pattern, fever, or recent shoe or activity change.
How quickly the area is changing and whether daily checks are being done.
careQuestions: Which findings suggest routine wound care discussion versus faster same-day review?
What daily monitoring details should be ready for the podiatry or diabetes visit?
precautions: Do not treat a worsening diabetic foot wound as a routine skin issue.
Do not ignore new numbness, drainage, or color change.

records.title: Kidney Function, Swelling, and Urine Change Review
records.summary: Organizes kidney follow-up using swelling, urine change, weight trend, blood pressure, diabetes context, hydration, recent labs, and medicine questions for a nephrology discussion.
records.safetyNotes: Breathing trouble, major swelling, confusion, very low urine output, chest symptoms, or rapidly worsening weakness needs prompt medical review.
whatToTrack: Recent creatinine, eGFR, potassium, urine protein wording, and weight change if available.
Blood pressure, swelling location, shortness of breath, and urine amount or color changes.
Hydration, vomiting, diarrhea, diabetes pattern, and recent medicine changes.
careQuestions: Which swelling and lab combinations suggest stable chronic monitoring versus faster nephrology review?
What home observations should be shared before the next kidney follow-up?
precautions: Do not change kidney-related or blood-pressure medicines from app guidance alone.
Do not ignore rapid swelling or low urine output.

records.title: Asthma Control, Night Waking, and Trigger Pattern Review
records.summary: Reviews asthma control using cough and wheeze timing, night waking, exercise limits, inhaler routine, triggers, peak-flow context, and rescue-relief pattern.
records.safetyNotes: Severe breathing trouble, inability to speak full sentences, blue lips, confusion, or poor relief from rescue medicine needs urgent real-world care.
whatToTrack: How often symptoms occur in the day, at night, or with exercise.
Trigger exposure, inhaler availability, spacer use, and how well rescue medicine works.
Any oxygen reading, cough severity, fever, chest tightness, or recent illness.
careQuestions: Does this pattern suggest poor day-to-day control, a flare, or urgent review?
Which control details should be shared during the respiratory follow-up?
precautions: Do not delay urgent care when breathing work is increasing or rescue medicine is not helping.
Do not stop controller therapy based on symptom improvement alone.

records.title: Chest Discomfort, Exertion, and Cardiac Safety Review
records.summary: Structures chest discomfort around exertion, pressure quality, palpitations, radiation, sweating, shortness of breath, nausea, and recovery pattern before selecting the safe next step.
records.safetyNotes: Chest pain with pressure, sweating, fainting, breathing trouble, arm or jaw pain, or sudden worsening should be treated as urgent medical care.
whatToTrack: When the discomfort starts, how long it lasts, and whether exertion or rest changes it.
Any sweating, nausea, breathlessness, palpitations, dizziness, or radiation to the arm, jaw, or back.
Past cardiac history, BP readings if available, and current heart-related medicines.
careQuestions: Which features suggest a routine follow-up topic and which suggest immediate medical review?
What should be documented for the cardiology visit if urgent signs are not present?
precautions: Do not use app guidance to explain away exertional chest symptoms.
Do not continue strenuous activity if symptoms are occurring.

records.title: Migraine Pattern versus Neurologic Red-Flag Review
records.summary: Helps separate a known migraine pattern from new neurologic warning signs using onset, aura, previous history, severity, vision change, weakness, speech, and recovery features.
records.safetyNotes: Sudden worst headache, new weakness, speech trouble, fainting, seizure, new confusion, or persistent vision loss needs urgent real-world assessment.
whatToTrack: Whether the headache matches prior migraine patterns or feels new in onset, location, or severity.
Any aura, nausea, light sensitivity, weakness, speech issues, fainting, or vision loss.
Trigger pattern, sleep loss, hydration, stress, menstrual timing, and medicine timing.
careQuestions: What makes this headache pattern consistent with prior migraine versus requiring neurologic assessment?
Which symptom changes should be documented for specialist follow-up?
precautions: Do not label a new neurologic symptom as migraine without real-world evaluation.
Do not ignore a sudden severe headache because of a prior migraine history.

records.title: Thyroid Medicine Timing and Lab Follow-up Review
records.summary: Organizes thyroid follow-up using medicine timing, missed tablets, TSH or T4 trends, fatigue, weight change, palpitations, temperature sensitivity, and pregnancy context.
records.safetyNotes: Chest pain, fainting, severe palpitations, major weakness, confusion, or pregnancy-related concern needs prompt clinician review.
whatToTrack: Exact medicine timing, missed doses, food timing, calcium or iron timing, and refill consistency.
TSH and thyroid hormone trends if available, plus fatigue, palpitations, constipation, or weight change.
Pregnancy status, temperature sensitivity, sleep, and mood changes.
careQuestions: Which timing or symptom issues should be reviewed before the next thyroid lab check?
What changes suggest this should move beyond routine follow-up?
precautions: Do not change thyroid medicine dose from this app.
Do not ignore palpitations, fainting, or major pregnancy-related questions.

records.title: Constipation, Abdominal Pain, and Bleeding Boundary Review
records.summary: Structures constipation concerns around stool pattern, abdominal pain, hydration, vomiting, blood, weight loss, medicines, and prior bowel history before follow-up planning.
records.safetyNotes: Severe abdominal pain, repeated vomiting, blood in stool, black stool, inability to pass gas, or major abdominal swelling needs prompt medical review.
whatToTrack: When the pattern started, last normal bowel movement, stool changes, and abdominal pain location.
Hydration, food pattern, new medicines, nausea, vomiting, blood, fever, and weight change.
Whether symptoms are improving, stable, or worsening over time.
careQuestions: Which bowel symptoms fit routine follow-up and which need faster evaluation?
What history details should be ready for a digestive review?
precautions: Do not treat bleeding or severe pain as routine constipation.
Do not ignore vomiting or inability to pass gas with worsening pain.

records.title: Snoring, Sleep Apnea, and Daytime Fatigue Review
records.summary: Builds a sleep-breathing review using snoring pattern, witnessed pauses, morning headache, daytime sleepiness, weight, blood pressure, and sleep schedule context.
records.safetyNotes: Severe daytime sleepiness affecting driving, new chest symptoms, fainting, or major breathing concern during sleep needs real-world review.
whatToTrack: Snoring severity, witnessed breathing pauses, waking unrefreshed, and daytime sleepiness.
Morning headaches, blood pressure pattern, weight change, and sleep schedule.
Any CPAP use, mask issues, or reasons the sleep routine is breaking down.
careQuestions: Which symptoms should prompt a sleep evaluation or CPAP follow-up discussion?
What home observations make the sleep review more useful?
precautions: Do not ignore severe daytime sleepiness that affects driving or work safety.
Do not rely on lifestyle guidance alone when breathing pauses are obvious.

records.title: Pregnancy Swelling, Headache, and Blood Pressure Boundary Review
records.summary: Organizes pregnancy-related swelling or headache questions using blood pressure, vision changes, upper abdominal pain, baby movement, and timing while keeping urgent maternal safety boundaries clear.
records.safetyNotes: Severe headache, vision change, very high blood pressure, heavy bleeding, reduced baby movement, chest pain, or breathing trouble needs urgent obstetric review.
whatToTrack: Pregnancy or postpartum timing, BP readings if available, swelling pattern, and headache onset.
Any vision change, upper abdominal pain, heavy bleeding, reduced baby movement, or breathlessness.
Hydration, sleep, recent clinician advice, and how quickly symptoms are changing.
careQuestions: Which maternal warning signs need immediate contact with the obstetric team or emergency care?
What details should be ready when asking for same-day pregnancy guidance?
precautions: Do not use routine headache guidance for pregnancy warning patterns.
Do not wait on app monitoring if fetal movement or severe symptoms change.

records.title: Anxiety, Sleep, and Daily Function Support Review
records.summary: Builds a structured anxiety review using triggers, sleep disruption, concentration, body symptoms, support systems, coping routine, and safety boundaries for worsening distress.
records.safetyNotes: Self-harm thoughts, inability to stay safe, severe confusion, chest pain, fainting, or rapidly worsening symptoms need urgent real-world help.
whatToTrack: Trigger pattern, body symptoms, sleep disruption, appetite, and concentration changes.
What support is available now, whether the person can function safely, and what coping steps help.
Any chest symptoms, fainting, suicidal thoughts, or rapidly worsening distress.
careQuestions: What makes this a routine support conversation versus a same-day safety issue?
Which function and sleep details should be shared during follow-up?
precautions: Do not dismiss dangerous thoughts or major functional collapse as routine anxiety.
Do not let mental-wellness framing hide chest pain, fainting, or breathing warning signs.

records.title: Blood Thinner, Bruising, and Bleeding Safety Review
records.summary: Organizes blood-thinner safety questions using bruising, bleeding, black stool, falls, missed doses, procedure timing, new medicines, and pharmacist or clinician follow-up needs.
records.safetyNotes: Head injury, major bleeding, black stool, vomiting blood, fainting, severe weakness, or breathing trouble needs urgent real-world medical care.
whatToTrack: Exact medicine name, recent bruising or bleeding pattern, falls, and missed or late doses.
Any new medicines, pain relievers, alcohol changes, planned procedures, or black stool.
Head injury, dizziness, severe weakness, or shortness of breath.
careQuestions: Which bleeding patterns can wait for a pharmacist or clinician call and which need urgent care?
What details should be ready before a blood-thinner follow-up or procedure discussion?
precautions: Do not decide blood-thinner dose changes from this app.
Do not ignore falls, head injury, or black stool.

records.title: HbA1c Trend, Meals, and Home Log Review
records.summary: Connects HbA1c review with meal timing, fasting and post-meal readings, illness, activity, medicines, and follow-up planning for diabetes care.
records.safetyNotes: Confusion, repeated vomiting, very high sugar symptoms, or very low sugar symptoms should be handled with prompt real-world review.
whatToTrack: Current HbA1c, fasting readings, post-meal readings, and how they compare with older results.
Meal pattern, activity, hydration, illness, and medicine timing around higher readings.
Any weight change, foot issues, sleep changes, or glucose symptoms.
careQuestions: Which log details make the diabetes follow-up more actionable than a single HbA1c value alone?
What additional home patterns should be reviewed before the next visit?
precautions: Do not use one HbA1c number alone to change medicines without clinician guidance.
Do not ignore symptoms just because the lab follow-up is already scheduled.

records.title: Post-Discharge Wound, Fever, and Recovery Review
records.summary: Builds a post-discharge check around wound change, fever, pain, drainage, new swelling, medicine changes, bowel or urine issues, and follow-up timing.
records.safetyNotes: Spreading redness, pus, fever, severe pain, breathing trouble, fainting, confusion, or discharge instructions not matching the current symptoms needs prompt medical review.
whatToTrack: Procedure or hospital date, wound appearance, fever pattern, and current pain level.
Medicine changes, bowel and urine status, swelling, drainage, and mobility changes.
What the discharge note said, when follow-up is booked, and what has changed since going home.
careQuestions: Which recovery findings fit expected monitoring and which need same-day contact with the care team?
What details should be added to the follow-up packet for the next visit?
precautions: Do not rely on routine wound monitoring if fever or drainage is worsening.
Do not ignore discharge mismatches when symptoms are escalating.

records.title: Vaccine Reaction, Fever, and Allergy Boundary Review
records.summary: Organizes common post-vaccine questions using timing, fever, soreness, rash, allergy history, breathing changes, hydration, and when to seek real-world review.
records.safetyNotes: Breathing trouble, facial swelling, severe rash, fainting, persistent confusion, or rapidly worsening symptoms after a vaccine needs urgent medical review.
whatToTrack: When the reaction started, which vaccine was given, and whether the symptoms are local or whole body.
Fever, rash, swelling, dizziness, breathing changes, hydration, and prior allergy history.
Whether symptoms are improving over hours, staying the same, or worsening.
careQuestions: Which reactions fit expected follow-up and which should move to urgent review?
What allergy or timing details should be shared if help is needed?
precautions: Do not ignore breathing changes or facial swelling because the shot was recent.
Do not rely on routine home care if symptoms are rapidly worsening.

records.title: Skin Infection, Redness, and Cellulitis Boundary Review
records.summary: Builds a skin-infection review using redness size, warmth, pain, swelling, drainage, fever, injury source, diabetes history, and change over time.
records.safetyNotes: Rapid spreading redness, fever, severe pain, facial involvement, confusion, streaking, or drainage with worsening symptoms needs prompt medical care.
whatToTrack: How large the red area is, whether it is warm, painful, swollen, or draining.
Any fever, injury, insect bite, diabetes, foot wound, or immune-risk condition.
Whether the redness is stable, spreading, or becoming more painful.
careQuestions: Which skin changes suggest routine review versus same-day or urgent assessment?
What timeline details should be documented for the clinician?
precautions: Do not minimize a spreading infection pattern.
Do not wait on routine follow-up when fever or rapidly increasing redness is present.
